name: Reply
classification_notes: The foundry describes Reply as a grotesk with near-monoline style, wide proportions and angular terminals, placing it squarely in the grotesque sans-serif tradition without allegiance to a specific historical model.
summary: A grotesque sans with wide proportions, angular terminals and open counters, designed to sit between plainness and mild eccentricity in text settings.
description: Rather than drawing on a single historical source, Reply synthesises qualities from a range of grotesks into a near-monoline typeface suited to both screen and print. Its wide proportions and large open counters aid legibility at text sizes, while angular terminals and occasional formal quirks prevent it from reading as entirely anonymous. The foundry frames it as an attempt to balance the unremarkable and the distinctive without tipping too far into either.
foundry_blurb: Approximate Type describe Reply as a typeface that appears ubiquitous at first glance, but reveals a subtle personality through wide proportions, angular terminals and occasional formal oddities. Not derived from any specific historic reference, it is framed as a synthesis of past grotesks that attempts to balance 'plain' and 'eccentric'. Reply is designed to perform as a text typeface in digital and printed environments, aided by its near-monoline construction and large, open counters.

name: Skanner
classification_notes: Skanner is a geometric sans with squared-off bowls, horizontal terminals and a constructed design language evoking sci-fi user interfaces; its display-oriented character count, 123 ligatures and strong stylistic personality place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A squared geometric sans with deep ink traps and 123 ligatures, built around a sci-fi aesthetic of VHS tapes, hoverbikes and retro-futurist interfaces.
description: Skanner's letterforms follow a strict geometric logic: squared bowls allow barcode-tight tracking, while horizontal terminals create natural conditions for an unusually large ligature set of 123 pairs. Deep ink traps introduce a subtle warmth that keeps the design from feeling purely mechanical. The foundry cites Eurostile as the obvious reference point, though Skanner brings a more rigorous, constructed design language of its own.
foundry_blurb: Approximate Type describe Skanner as a typeface hardcoded with the aesthetic of commercial cargo vessels, worn VHS tapes and sci-fi user interfaces. Its squared bowls enable tight tracking, horizontal terminals invite ligature formation, and 123 ligatures are included. Though not based on any specific reference, Eurostile is noted as a natural comparison, with Skanner offering a more constructed design language and deep ink traps that add warmth.

name: Canopy
classification_notes: A single-weight display typeface with heavy ornamentation and stylistic flair, explicitly described as such by the foundry and designed for a specific exhibition context rather than continuous text use.
summary: A single-weight ornamental display face commissioned for a Symbolist art exhibition at the National Gallery of Denmark in 2018, drawing on fin-de-siècle lettering traditions.
description: Designed for the exhibition Hieroglyphs – Symbolist Drawings 1890-1910 at the National Gallery of Denmark, the typeface takes loose inspiration from the cover lettering of Praktisk Tegnekursus (1900), a drawing manual popular among Symbolist artists of the period. Flair is built into every letterform, with stylistic alternates, ornamental ligatures and oldstyle figures extending the expressive range of an otherwise single-weight family. The result suits editorial and cultural contexts where the mood of late nineteenth-century mysticism and natural symbolism is deliberately invoked.
foundry_blurb: Approximate Type describe Canopy as unapologetically a one-weight display typeface with a severe amount of flair and ornamentation, originally designed for a Symbolist exhibition at the National Gallery of Denmark in 2018. They note that its shapes are "pregnant with meaning", in keeping with the Symbolists' fascination with dreams, mysticism and the natural world, and that it draws loose inspiration from the lettering on the cover of Praktisk Tegnekursus (1900).

name: Stick
classification_notes: A single-weight geometric display typeface with a distinctive construction rule applied at all intersecting strokes, making it firmly display in character and purpose.
summary: A one-weight geometric display face built on a strict rule that sharpens every stroke intersection to near breaking point, mimicking the physical act of joining pieces of wood.
description: Each intersection in Stick is drawn with an inward sharpening that the foundry describes as a primitive ink-trap concept, merging a physical construction principle with a digital implementation. The result reads as both brutalist and handmade at large sizes, while producing an unusual texture at smaller settings that makes short paragraphs viable. Coverage includes Latin Extended with accented characters across Central and Northern European languages, arrows and a small set of ligatures.
foundry_blurb: Approximate Type describe Stick as a witch's charm, a bonfire or a toothpick — a one-weight geometric display typeface that mimics manually adhering pieces of wood together. Its formal rule dictates that wherever two lines intersect, they must be sharpened to the point of almost snapping, which they frame as a primitive ink-trap concept merging physical construction with digital drawing. Best suited to headlines, it also produces an interesting texture at smaller sizes that supports short paragraphs.

name: Clerk
classification_notes: A single-weight display typeface with deliberate decorative gaps that exceed functional stencil requirements, rooted in early twentieth-century architectural lettering. Its modular, ornamental construction places it firmly in the display category with a stencil-adjacent character.
summary: A one-weight display typeface revived from 1920s Dutch architectural lettering, in which deliberate gaps transform a stencil logic into surface ornament.
description: Based on facade lettering attributed to Dutch architect Samuel de Clerq, drawn in the 1920s for the savings bank of The Hague, the typeface treats its gaps not as functional stencil breaks but as decorative texture. Bowl-ligatures across both upper and lowercase, along with a flat-top figure eight, add specificity and period character. Its modular construction lends itself to headline work, animation and more experimental dismantling of letterforms.
foundry_blurb: Approximate Type describe Clerk as a one-weight display typeface in which each letter carries more gaps than strictly necessary, tipping stencil function into ornament. They trace it to a piece of lettering likely drawn by Dutch architect Samuel de Clerq for the facade of the Nutsspaarbank in The Hague during the 1920s, a building he designed. The typeface includes an array of bowl-ligatures and a flat-top eight, and the foundry note its modular construction suits animation and dismantling alongside titling and headline use.

name: Medley
classification_notes: Medley blends soft, pooling serifs derived from Vietnamese calligraphy with the geometric construction of French Art Deco and Russian Constructivism, resulting in a decorative display serif that defies straightforward classification.
summary: A display serif built from the collision of Cooper Black's warmth, Vietnamese calligraphic terminals and the geometric rigour of Art Deco and Constructivist letterforms.
description: Inspired by the prevalence of Cooper Black on Vietnamese street signage, Medley synthesises several distinct visual traditions: the soft, pooling stroke terminals of Vietnamese calligraphy, the geometric structure of French Art Deco and Russian Constructivism, and an undercurrent of Danish design sensibility. The result is a hybrid typeface that sits comfortably in both Eastern and Western contexts without being fully anchored in either. Extensive Vietnamese diacritic coverage makes it genuinely usable for the Chữ Quốc Ngữ writing system, which is rare for a display face of this character.
foundry_blurb: Approximate Type describe Medley as an unlikely hybrid born from a trip to Vietnam and curiosity about Cooper Black's prolific use in Vietnamese street signage. The typeface synthesises French Art Deco, Russian Constructivism and Vietnamese calligraphic terminals, with a subconscious Danish influence from its designer, producing a decorative face suited to large display sizes while retaining legibility at smaller scales.

name: Aguzzo
classification_notes: Aguzzo combines geometric construction with transitional traits, drawing from the engravers-style Roman capitals tradition of Butti and Novarese's Augustea (1951). The steep 16° italic inclination and triangular punctuation alternate set place it firmly in the transitional serif camp with display-leaning character.
summary: A contemporary take on Butti and Novarese's Augustea, blending geometric construction with transitional serif traits and a steep 16° italic.
description: Aguzzo draws explicitly from Fonderia Nebiolo's Augustea (1951), the engravers-style typeface rooted in Roman capitals, adapting its triangular punctuation as an alternate character set. The Roman style carries a sharp, geometric quality while the italic's 16° inclination and broad-nib flourishes introduce contrast between the two styles. The combination of occasionally unusual letterforms with high legibility makes it usable across a range of sizes and contexts.
foundry_blurb: Approximate Type describe Aguzzo as a typeface that combines geometry with transitional traits, designed as a contemporary nod to Alessandro Butti and Aldo Novarese's Augustea from 1951. The triangular punctuation characteristic of Augustea is preserved as an alternate character set, the Roman's sharp expression is echoed by the steep 16° italic, and broad-nib flourishes add contrast between styles. Despite its occasionally unusual letterforms, Aguzzo is intended to offer a high degree of legibility across multiple sizes and contexts.
